rounds.position_summary: More likely than not a court finds both halves of the compound proposition true: (1) the councilmembers' Signal messages about franchise terms, diversion targets, "what council will accept," and RFP timing are "public records" under the content-controls-not-location rule of City of San Jose v. Superior Court, and (2) the clerk's "no responsive records" answer — issued after searching only city systems, with no inquiry to the three individual councilmembers about personal-device/account communications, despite a request expressly covering "any platform or account" — falls short of the reasonably-calculated-search standard that San Jose and its Court of Appeal remand decision require, and thus violated the Act. The compound (conjunctive) nature of the proposition and some residual doctrinal wiggle room on "used/retained" and on how courts characterize inadequate-search liability keep this from being a near-certainty.
rationales: Gov. Code § 7920.530 (former § 6252(e)) defines 'public records' by content and function ('relating to the conduct of the public's business,' 'prepared, owned, used, or retained') without reference to the account or device on which a writing sits, and the messages described — franchise terms, diversion targets, political vote-counting, RFP timing — sit squarely within that functional definition.

City of San Jose v. Superior Court (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608 directly rejected a categorical exemption for city officials' communications on personal accounts/devices and held that content, not location, is the touchstone, making this fact pattern (officials discussing franchise business on personal phones) close to the paradigm case the opinion was written to address.

The CPRA's duty-to-assist and reasonable-search obligations (Gov. Code § 7922.600, former § 6253.1; see also Community Youth Athletic Center v. City of National City (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 1385 on search adequacy) require an inquiry reasonably calculated to locate records, and where the request expressly demanded communications 'on any platform or account,' confining the search to city servers is a strong candidate for a per-se inadequate search under the Court of Appeal's remand decision in San Jose (2018) 24 Cal.App.5th 1013, which contemplated that agencies must at least ask custodians to search personal accounts.

Existence of ~40 surviving screenshots supplies concrete evidentiary proof of content and participation that removes speculative-record problems a court might otherwise use to avoid reaching the public-record question, making a definitive (rather than remanded/deferred) holding on prong one more likely here than in a case with no surviving evidence.

The proposition is conjunctive and thus more fragile than either prong alone; a court could plausibly find the messages qualify as public records yet still excuse the initial search as facially reasonable given ambiguity (as of the response date) about whether the CPRA reaches messages on a third-party-hosted app like Signal, which would defeat the proposition even though prong one succeeds.

rounds.biggest_uncertainty: Whether a court treats these consultant-initiated, ephemeral Signal messages as sufficiently 'prepared, owned, used, or retained' by the councilmembers in their official capacity to satisfy San Jose's test — as opposed to characterizing them as informal, quasi-political/lobbying chatter outside the CPRA's reach — since San Jose itself involved the official's own texts/emails rather than a third-party-hosted group chat, and no published California decision squarely addresses a private bidder's consultant-run disappearing-message platform.

rounds.position_summary: A court applying City of San Jose v. Superior Court would very likely hold that the Signal messages — discussions by sitting councilmembers of franchise terms, diversion targets, "what council will realistically accept," and RFP timing — meet the content-based definition of "public records" under Gov. Code § 6252(e) regardless of their location on personal devices/accounts, and would very likely find the clerk's "no responsive records" answer deficient because the city never inquired of the three councilmembers about personal-account communications before answering, a search-adequacy failure San Jose's own remand contemplated. The proposition is well-supported but not certain, mainly because the auto-deletion of most messages complicates how definitively a court can characterize the destroyed content versus the ~40 preserved screenshots.
rationales: San Jose (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608 makes public-records status turn on whether content 'relates to the conduct of the public's business,' not on the account or device where it is stored, and the stipulated subject matter here (franchise terms, diversion targets, political feasibility, RFP timing) sits at the core of that test rather than its margins.

Because three sitting councilmembers were direct participants discussing a matter pending before their own body, this presents the strong-content-nexus scenario San Jose treated as clearly covered, unlike the harder edge cases (e.g., low-level staff or third-party communications) the Court left more open.

San Jose's remand instructions and the CPRA duty-to-assist framework (Gov. Code § 6253.1) require agencies to make a reasonably calculated inquiry into likely repositories of records, including asking individual officials to check personal accounts when there is reason to believe public business was discussed there; searching only 'city systems' without any such inquiry falls short of that standard as a matter of law.

The ~40 surviving screenshots alone are direct, tangible evidence that responsive material existed outside city systems at the time of the request, making the 'no responsive records' answer factually inaccurate independent of any harder characterization dispute about the deleted messages.

Some residual uncertainty exists because a court may distinguish between declaring the surviving screenshots public records (clear) and formally characterizing the auto-deleted, unrecoverable messages as having 'been' public records (murkier, resting on witness recollection and inference rather than the writings themselves), which could lead a careful court to hedge the scope of its holding even while finding a search-adequacy violation.

rounds.position_summary: A court would very likely find the Signal messages qualified as "public records" under City of San Jose (squarely on-point precedent on personal-device communications about public business), and would somewhat less certainly but still probably find that the clerk's "no responsive records" response violated the Act, because the search never reached the councilmembers' personal accounts and because roughly 40 known-to-exist screenshots were never located or produced at all.
rationales: City of San Jose v. Superior Court (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608 held that writings concerning the conduct of public business remain public records regardless of storage on personal devices/accounts, and franchise-term negotiations, diversion targets, and RFP timing plainly concern the conduct of public business, making the substantive 'public record' determination close to settled on these facts.

The clerk's search was confined to city systems and never reached out to the three councilmembers about personal-account communications, which is hard to reconcile with San Jose's remand direction and with the post-San Jose statutory codification (AB 700, now generally cited as Gov. Code section 7922.545 - verify exact number) requiring agencies to route requests reasonably likely to implicate personal accounts to the individual official for a diligent search.

The undisputed existence of ~40 surviving screenshots in a councilmember's possession that were neither searched for nor disclosed is itself strong evidence that 'no responsive records' was factually inaccurate, independent of the harder legal question about the already-deleted messages.

CPRA declaratory and injunctive relief (Gov. Code section 7923.100, formerly section 6259) does not require that responsive records presently exist, so a court can declare the deleted messages were public records and that the response process violated the Act even though most content is now unrecoverable, which supports finding a violation despite the auto-deletion.

Countervailing uncertainty: the city could argue the clerk had no actual or constructive knowledge of the Signal group's existence at the time of the CPRA response, and no controlling appellate decision has yet defined precisely how much affirmative inquiry into elected officials' ephemeral personal messaging apps 'reasonable search' requires, giving a trial court room to find the search adequate under the circumstances then known.

rounds.biggest_uncertainty: Whether a court will treat the duty to inquire into councilmembers' personal accounts as automatically triggered by a broadly worded CPRA request (making the clerk's failure to ask a per se violation), or will instead excuse the clerk's search as reasonable absent proof the clerk had actual or constructive notice of the Signal group at the time of the response - this precise 'trigger' question has not been definitively resolved by binding published California appellate authority since San Jose.

rounds.position_summary: Starting from the strongest lawful-conduct position — that the clerk reasonably searched "city systems" and had no actual knowledge of a private Signal channel — that defense mostly collapses on these facts. It is very likely a reviewing court holds the Signal content was a "public record" under San Jose (content, not device, controls), and very likely finds the "no responsive records" answer violated the CPRA, both because the request itself demanded "any platform or account" (giving actual notice that a city-systems-only search was insufficient) and because at least one councilmember concededly still held ~40 responsive screenshots when the answer was given, making "no responsive records" false as to existing material. The compound proposition is strong but not certain because CPRA search-duty doctrine for personal-device records remains somewhat unsettled post-San Jose, and a court could still characterize the clerk's initial ignorance of the group's existence as a reasonable search "at the time," blunting (though not eliminating) a violation finding.
rationales: San Jose holds that the content of a writing, not the device, account, or personal ownership, determines whether it is a "public record," and franchise terms, diversion targets, and RFP timing are core "conduct of the public's business," so the Signal discussions very likely qualify.

The CPRA request expressly sought communications "on any platform or account," which gave the clerk actual notice that a search confined to city servers could not satisfy the Act's good-faith reasonable-search standard.

The "no responsive records" answer was demonstrably inaccurate the moment it was given, since one councilmember still possessed roughly 40 screenshots of responsive material, which alone likely establishes a violation independent of the broader public-record/search-methodology questions.

San Jose remanded specifically for development of a reasonable search methodology, and emerging post-remand practice generally requires agencies to inquire of individual custodians once on notice that personal-device records may exist, so a search with zero inquiry to any councilmember falls short of that developing standard.

Countervailing uncertainty remains because a court could find some or all content is preliminary/deliberative and thus exempt from disclosure even though it is a "public record," and could excuse the clerk's initial search as reasonable given no proof the clerk had actual knowledge of the Signal group before this dispute, which would soften a violation finding on the search-adequacy prong even if it does not eliminate it.

rounds.biggest_uncertainty: Whether California courts will treat "zero inquiry to individual officials" as per se unreasonable search under the CPRA absent a settled bright-line duty to canvass personal devices — the precise scope of the post-San Jose search-methodology obligation is still doctrinally underdeveloped, so a court could instead frame this as a forward-looking remedial issue rather than a clear violation at the time the response was given.

rounds.position_summary: More likely than not a reviewing court finds both halves of the proposition true: the Signal content is "public records" under San Jose because it substantively concerns pending public business (franchise terms, diversion targets, RFP timing) regardless of the personal device/app used, and the clerk's "no responsive records" answer — reached by searching only city systems despite a request explicitly covering "any platform or account" and despite ~40 screenshots known to still exist — falls short of the reasonably-calculated-search standard San Jose contemplates, making the response a CPRA violation. The weaker link is the second prong, since San Jose expressly left search methodology for remand rather than adopting a bright-line duty-to-inquire rule, so a court could excuse a clerk who had no actual knowledge of the group at the time of the initial response.
rationales: San Jose holds that writings concerning the conduct of public business do not lose their public character merely because they were sent or received on a personal account, and the Signal content described (franchise terms, diversion targets, what council will accept, RFP timing) is squarely business-related rather than personal or purely political chatter.

Because three of five councilmembers — acting as public officials on a matter of pending city business — participated, the 'prepared, owned, used, or retained by' element of Gov. Code § 7920.530 (former § 6252(e)) is satisfied under San Jose's agency-law reasoning regardless of the device or app used.

Pacific Refuse's request expressly asked for records 'on any platform or account,' giving the clerk direct notice that personal-account communications were in scope, so confining the search to city systems alone is unlikely to satisfy the 'reasonably calculated to uncover all responsive documents' standard courts apply to CPRA search adequacy.

At least ~40 screenshots indisputably still exist in a councilmember's possession and were apparently never sought, so 'no responsive records' was not merely under-researched but arguably factually false as to existing, retrievable records — a violation independent of the auto-deletion issue.

Countervailing factor: San Jose expressly remanded rather than resolving search-methodology questions, so whether a clerk who had no actual knowledge of the Signal group at the time of response had an affirmative duty to inquire of each councilmember is a genuinely unsettled application of the doctrine, which could support a good-faith-search defense and lower the probability the response is deemed an outright violation.
rounds.biggest_uncertainty: Whether a court treats the clerk's apparent lack of actual knowledge of the Signal group at the time of the CPRA response as excusing (versus aggravating) the failure to inquire of individual councilmembers, since San Jose did not adopt a bright-line duty-to-ask rule and left search-adequacy standards for case-by-case development.

rounds.position_summary: More likely than not (and fairly strongly so) that a court finds both halves of the conjunctive proposition true: the Signal messages were "public records" under the City of San Jose framework, and the clerk's city-systems-only search, never reaching out to the three councilmembers despite a request explicitly covering "any platform or account," fell short of the Act's reasonable-search obligation. The strongest single data point is the undisclosed 40 surviving screenshots — a concrete, known-to-exist responsive record that a proper inquiry would have surfaced.
rationales: City of San Jose v. Superior Court (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608 holds a writing relating to conduct of public business, retained by an individual official on a personal account, satisfies Gov. Code § 6252(e); messages about franchise terms, diversion targets, 'what council will realistically accept,' and RFP timing are arguably a cleaner fit for that test than San Jose's own mixed personal/official texts.

A majority bloc (three of five councilmembers) discussing deal terms on a pending $180M award goes to the heart of 'conduct of the public's business,' strengthening both the substantive-nexus and the 'retained by' prongs San Jose construed broadly to reach individual custodians acting in their official capacity.

Gov. Code § 6252.5 (added by AB 700, eff. 2022) specifically requires agencies to contact officials and can require a signed declaration when a request could reasonably encompass personal-account communications by elected officials, and Pacific Refuse's request expressly demanded records 'on any platform or account,' making a city-systems-only search facially non-compliant with both that statute and the general 'reasonably calculated' search standard.

The one councilmember's 40 surviving screenshots were known (at least to that member) to exist at the time of the response, so a search that never asked any councilmember anything is close to a per se inadequate search independent of how the broader legal test is resolved.

Countervailing factor: San Jose expressly left open how agencies should structure searches of personal accounts and did not address records already destroyed before a request arrives, so a court could still find the messages were public records while treating the 'no responsive records' answer as a closer, more fact-bound question rather than an automatic statutory violation — this tempers full confidence in the conjunctive proposition as stated.

rounds.position_summary: A court would very likely hold both halves of the proposition true: (1) the Signal messages were "public records" under San Jose's content-based test because they concerned franchise terms, diversion targets, "what council will realistically accept," and RFP timing — core public-business content indistinguishable from the personal-device texts/emails San Jose held were public records regardless of storage location; and (2) the clerk's "no responsive records" answer, issued after searching only city systems and never asking any councilmember to search personal devices/accounts, fell short of the reasonable-diligence search standard the CPRA requires — especially since the request explicitly sought records "on any platform or account" and at least 40 extant screenshots were never located or produced. The compound proposition is not a close call on either prong individually, but real uncertainty remains on the precise legal contours of the "duty to inquire" (as distinct from the duty to disclose records once found), which is less definitively fixed by binding appellate authority than the public-record-content question, and on how a court treats the fact that most messages were already gone before the request arrived.
rationales: San Jose (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608 makes content, not custody or device location, the touchstone for 'public record' status under Gov. Code § 6252(e), and discussion of franchise terms, diversion targets, and RFP timing is core public-business content that maps almost exactly onto San Jose's own fact pattern of personal-device texts/emails about city business.

The CPRA request expressly sought councilmember communications 'on any platform or account,' which put the clerk on direct notice that personal-account records were within scope, so a search confined to city systems is facially inadequate under the reasonable-diligence standard the Act requires for locating responsive records.

At least 40 screenshots indisputably existed in a councilmember's possession at (and presumably after) the time of the response, so independent of the disputed status of the auto-deleted messages, the city demonstrably failed to locate and produce records that did exist, which is a comparatively clean basis for a violation finding.

San Jose remanded without fully resolving search-methodology standards but signaled that agencies may need to reach out to individual officials/employees regarding personal accounts, and post-2017 municipal best-practice guidance (e.g., League of California Cities model policies) treats 'ask the record-holder to search their own device' as baseline compliance, making a total omission of that step an easier case for a court to fault.

The involvement of three of five councilmembers in sustained substantive policy discussion (diversion targets, what council will accept) independently corroborates that the content was governmental deliberation rather than personal chatter, reinforcing the public-record characterization and undercutting any argument the messages were merely informal or preliminary in a way that would exempt them.

rounds.position_summary: Revising upward from 70 to 77. The panel's Theme D point is the one I underweighted in Round 1 and now find decisive: the ~40 screenshots were preserved by the uneasy councilmember before auto-deletion, and nothing in the facts suggests they were later destroyed, so they almost certainly still existed on a personal device at the time Pacific Refuse's CPRA request was made and answered. That means the clerk's "no responsive records" answer was not merely the product of an arguably-reasonable-but-narrow search — it was factually false as to a known, existing, non-hypothetical set of responsive franchise-related writings, independent of the harder question of what happens to the auto-deleted messages. That is a cleaner, more certain violation theory than the search-methodology debate I leaned on in Round 1. Combined with the strong, largely undisputed case that San Jose's content-based test reaches franchise-related group-chat content regardless of who hosts the platform, both prongs of the conjunctive proposition clear "more likely than not" by a wider margin than I credited before.
rationales: Gov. Code § 7920.530 (former § 6252(e)) and City of San Jose v. Superior Court (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608 establish that content and function, not the device/account/platform on which a writing sits, determine public-record status; franchise terms, diversion targets, 'what council will accept,' and RFP timing are core conduct-of-the-public's-business content that a councilmember 'uses' by receiving and participating in the discussion, regardless of who created the Signal channel — this undercuts my Round 1 hesitation (Theme L) that the consultant-hosted, third-party-platform facts meaningfully distinguish this case from San Jose's own facts.

The surviving ~40 screenshots supply an independent, low-uncertainty basis for finding the 'no responsive records' answer inaccurate: unlike disappearing messages, screenshots are static and nothing in the facts indicates they were destroyed before the CPRA response, so at the moment the clerk answered, responsive records almost certainly existed on a personal device the clerk never searched — this is a stronger and more certain violation theory than relying solely on unsettled search-adequacy doctrine for the deleted messages.

The request's express language ('on any platform or account') gave the clerk at least constructive notice that a city-systems-only search was insufficient, and confining the search to city servers in the face of that language is a strong candidate for inadequate search under the duty-to-assist/reasonable-search line of authority (Gov. Code § 7922.600; Community Youth Athletic Center v. City of National City (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 1385; and, more contestably, the San Jose remand decision, 24 Cal.App.5th 1013, on which the panel's Disagreement Point 2 shows genuine doctrinal uncertainty).

Three of five councilmembers (a voting majority) substantively participating in deliberation over franchise terms strengthens both the public-business nexus for prong one and the argument that the clerk should have anticipated personal-device records existed, cutting against a 'no actual/constructive knowledge' defense (Theme G) — even a generic duty-to-assist inquiry to councilmembers, which the clerk skipped entirely, would likely have surfaced the group's existence.

The proposition remains conjunctive and therefore more fragile than either prong alone, and genuine unresolved doctrine remains on (a) whether a court treats the search failure as a per se violation versus a fact-bound adequacy question allowing a good-faith defense, and (b) whether a court confines any 'public records' holding to the surviving screenshots and treats the bulk of destroyed messages as a retention/spoliation matter outside the CPRA determination itself (Theme I) — both cut somewhat against full confidence even after the upward revision.

rounds.response_to_panel: Theme D moved me most: six of seven panelists treated the surviving ~40 screenshots as an independent, near-certain basis for finding the 'no responsive records' answer false/violative, distinct from the harder question of what to do about the deleted messages. In Round 1 I used the screenshots only as evidence supporting the public-records characterization (prong one) and didn't give them full weight as a standalone violation theory for prong two; that was an underweighting on my part, and correcting it pushes my estimate up several points. Theme H (majority-bloc participation) and the panel's general treatment of the platform/ephemeral-app distinction as immaterial (contra my Round 1 Theme L concern) also moved me — on reflection the 'used' prong of San Jose doesn't turn on who owns the chat software. I did not fully adopt the panel median (78) because Disagreement Points 1–2 and 6 persuade me the search-methodology doctrine genuinely is unsettled post-San Jose (I'm not convinced the 2018 remand decision fixes a clear personal-account-inquiry rule), and because the conjunctive structure of the proposition still carries real fragility — so I land at 77, just under the median rather than at or above it.

rounds.position_summary: A court would very likely hold the Signal messages were "public records" under San Jose's content-based test (the subject matter — franchise terms, diversion targets, political feasibility, RFP timing, discussed by a voting-majority bloc of three councilmembers — is core public business regardless of platform or device), and would very likely also find the clerk's city-systems-only search inadequate given a request that expressly demanded records "on any platform or account." I moderate slightly from my Round 1 score of 80 to 78 because reconsideration of the panel's Theme G (a plausible, non-frivolous defense that the clerk lacked actual/constructive knowledge of the Signal group and so the initial search was "reasonable at the time") and the genuine doctrinal split in Disagreement Point 1 (whether request language alone triggers a duty to inquire, versus knowledge being required) persuade me the second prong carries slightly more real litigation risk than I initially weighted — though I still think that defense is more likely to lose than win against the "any platform or account" language.
rationales: San Jose (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608 ties 'public record' status to content ('relates to the conduct of the public's business') rather than to the account, device, or platform where the writing sits, and the stipulated subject matter here — franchise terms, diversion targets, 'what council will realistically accept,' RFP timing — is a paradigm case rather than a marginal one, especially with a voting-majority bloc of three councilmembers as direct participants (Theme A/H, 7/7 and 3/7 panelists respectively).

The CPRA request's explicit 'on any platform or account' language, combined with the duty-to-assist/reasonable-diligence standard (Gov. Code former § 6253.1 / § 7922.600; see also ACLU of Northern California v. Superior Court (2011) 202 Cal.App.4th 55 on 'reasonably calculated to locate responsive documents'), plausibly obligated the clerk to inquire of the named councilmembers about personal-account communications independent of whether the clerk had specific prior knowledge of the Signal group — a request-language-triggers-duty theory that 5/7 panelists endorsed (Theme C) and that I still find more persuasive than the competing knowledge-required theory.

The ~40 surviving screenshots are direct, tangible evidence that responsive material existed at the time of the request, making 'no responsive records' factually inaccurate independent of any harder characterization dispute about the destroyed messages — an essentially undisputed point across the panel (Theme D).

On reconsideration, Theme G's countervailing point (3/7 panelists: a court could find the initial search reasonable if the clerk had no actual or constructive notice of the private, consultant-run, ephemeral Signal group) and the unresolved doctrinal split in Disagreement Point 1 over what triggers the duty to inquire are legitimate sources of litigation risk I underweighted in Round 1; this is why I moved from 80 to 78 rather than holding at 80, even though I believe this defense is more likely to fail than succeed given the request's explicit multi-platform language.

The proposition is conjunctive (public-record status AND a violation from the no-inquiry response), and while I now peg each prong individually higher than my blended estimate might suggest (roughly ~90% for the public-records prong, ~85% for the search-violation prong), positive correlation between the two findings (a court persuaded of strong public-business content nexus is also more likely to be unsympathetic to a shortcut search) keeps the combined estimate in the high-70s rather than materially lower.

rounds.response_to_panel: I moved from 80 to 78 based on Theme G (clerk's lack of actual/constructive knowledge as a countervailing defense, raised by 3/7 panelists) together with Disagreement Point 1 (the unresolved doctrinal split over whether request-language alone or actual/constructive knowledge triggers the duty to inquire of individual custodians). On reflection I think these are legitimate, non-frivolous considerations I underweighted in Round 1 — they represent a real, if likely losing, defense for the city on the second (search-violation) prong. I did not defer to the panel median (78) merely for consensus's sake; I re-derived 78 independently by separately estimating the two conjunctive prongs (~90% and ~85%) and combining them with positive correlation.

rounds.position_summary: A court would very likely hold the Signal messages were "public records" under City of San Jose's content-controls-not-location test (franchise terms, diversion targets, RFP timing, and "what council will accept" are core public business, and a voting-majority bloc of three councilmembers substantively participated), and would more likely than not also hold that the clerk's "no responsive records" response violated the Act, because the request's explicit "any platform or account" language put the city on notice that a city-systems-only search was insufficient, no inquiry was ever routed to the individual councilmembers, and roughly 40 surviving screenshots demonstrably responsive to the request were never located or produced. The weaker link remains the search-adequacy prong, given the absence of binding appellate authority precisely fixing what triggers the duty to inquire of individual officials about personal-account communications.
rationales: City of San Jose v. Superior Court (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608 makes the 'public record' characterization close to settled on these facts; if anything this scenario is a cleaner fit than San Jose's own facts because it involves a sustained, multi-member deliberative exchange among a voting-majority bloc (3 of 5) about a specific $180M procurement, which strengthens both the 'concerning the conduct of public business' and 'prepared, used, or retained by a public official' elements relative to San Jose's individual-employee-text facts.

The request's express 'on any platform or account' language is a strong, independent basis for finding the city-systems-only search inadequate: it forecloses any argument that the city lacked notice personal accounts were in scope, which is precisely the gap AB 700 (Stats. 2021, ch. 132 or 300 - verify) was designed to close by requiring agencies to forward such requests to individual officials/employees for a diligent personal-account search; on this reading the duty to inquire is triggered by request scope, not by the clerk's actual/constructive knowledge of the specific Signal group.

The undisputed existence of ~40 surviving screenshots in a councilmember's possession, never searched for or disclosed, independently establishes that 'no responsive records' was factually false at the moment it was given -- a violation that does not depend on resolving the harder legal question of whether the already-deleted messages retained public-record status, and that a court could find even if it treated the deleted-message body more cautiously.

The proposition's phrasing ('were public records... concerning the franchise') most naturally reads as a legal characterization of the communications as a class, similar to how San Jose itself announced a governing principle rather than adjudicating each individual text; I do not think a court confining detailed remedial relief to the 40 surviving screenshots would necessarily decline to characterize the broader Signal exchange as having been public records while it existed, so the 'scope-narrowing' concern raised by some analyses goes more to remedy than to the characterization question the proposition actually poses.

Countervailing uncertainty that keeps me from going higher: there is no binding published appellate decision squarely holding that a consultant-initiated, third-party-hosted, auto-deleting group chat triggers the same search obligations as San Jose's personal texts/emails, and a trial court could plausibly credit a good-faith, reasonable-search defense for the pre-litigation response given the clerk's lack of actual knowledge of the group's existence, particularly since the exact statutory trigger for the AB 700 personal-account-inquiry duty (request-scope alone vs. some threshold of agency awareness) remains doctrinally unsettled.

rounds.biggest_uncertainty: Whether the duty to inquire of individual councilmembers about personal-account communications is triggered automatically by a broadly worded CPRA request (making the city-systems-only search a clean violation), or instead requires some showing of the clerk's actual or constructive knowledge of the specific Signal group before the search can be deemed unreasonable -- this precise trigger question lacks controlling published California appellate authority post-San Jose, and the panel itself could not agree on the correct statutory citation or content of the AB 700 provision that might resolve it.
rounds.response_to_panel: Two panel considerations genuinely moved me, modestly, from 76 to 78. First, Theme C (the request's explicit "any platform or account" language as notice) sharpened my own reasoning: on reflection I think this language is best read as triggering the AB 700-style duty to route the request to individual officials regardless of the clerk's independent knowledge of the Signal group, which pushes the search-adequacy prong somewhat higher than I credited in round 1, where I treated the knowledge question as more central. Second, Theme H (majority-bloc participation) reinforced my confidence on the 'public record' prong, since three of five members deliberating together is a materially stronger public-business nexus than San Jose's own facts. I did not adopt Theme I's scope-narrowing concern (confining any holding to the 40 screenshots) as a reason to lower my number, because I read the proposition as posing a characterization question the general exchange, not a remedy question, and I think a court would resolve the characterization question for the class of communications much as San Jose did. I also declined to adopt the 2018 Court of Appeal citation offered by one panelist (24 Cal.App.5th 1013) without being able to verify it, and flagged the AB 700 section-number dispute (Theme/Disagreement 5) as unresolved rather than picking a side. I stayed within the panel's tight range rather than anchoring to the median mechanically -- 78 reflects my own reconstructed prong-by-prong estimate (~90% public-record prong x ~83-85% search-violation prong, adjusted upward for correlation between the two findings) rather than deference to the group's central tendency.

rounds.position_summary: Reconsidering independently, the compound proposition holds up slightly better than my round-1 estimate. Prong one (public-record status) is very strong: San Jose's content-controls-not-location rule squarely covers franchise terms, diversion targets, and RFP timing, and the fact that three of five councilmembers — a voting majority — were jointly discussing "what council will realistically accept" makes this look more like collective conduct of public business than San Jose's own facts (individual staffer texts), not less. Prong two (violation) is also strong, not primarily because of an unsettled search-methodology doctrine, but because the "no responsive records" answer was objectively false the moment it was given: a councilmember held ~40 responsive screenshots at that time, regardless of whether the clerk's city-systems-only search was independently unreasonable. That factual-falsity route to a violation finding is less doctrinally fragile than the search-adequacy route and is largely immune to the "clerk lacked actual knowledge" defense, since the existence of responsive material — not the clerk's diligence — is what makes the response inaccurate. I remain short of the 80s because a residual, non-trivial chance exists that a court (a) treats deliberative/preliminary-draft exemptions as covering some content even though it's a public record, (b) confines any public-record holding to the surviving screenshots and routes the deleted bulk into a retention/spoliation lane rather than a clean CPRA violation, or (c) is persuaded that the consultant-hosted, third-party ephemeral platform presents a genuinely open question San Jose didn't resolve.
rationales: San Jose's content-based test (2 Cal.5th 608) is doctrinally settled and squarely reached by these facts; the panel's unanimous reliance on this theme confirms it as the least contestable part of the analysis, so prong one alone likely clears 85-90%.

The majority-bloc fact (3 of 5 councilmembers, a Brown Act-quorum-sized group, discussing 'what council will accept') strengthens the public-business characterization beyond San Jose's own facts — this is not personal chatter among staff but coordinated engagement by a governing-body majority on matters within their official authority, which I underweighted in round 1.

The ~40 surviving screenshots provide an independent, fact-based route to a violation finding that sidesteps the harder search-methodology doctrine entirely: 'no responsive records' was false as stated at the time it was given, and that falsity does not depend on resolving whether zero-inquiry-to-custodians is per se unreasonable under an unsettled post-San Jose standard.

Countervailing risk persists on scope: a court could confine any 'public records' holding to the provable surviving screenshots and treat the deleted majority of messages as a retention/spoliation question rather than folding them wholesale into the CPRA violation finding, which would make the proposition's sweeping phrasing ('the councilmembers' Signal messages... were public records') only partially correct as to the deleted portion.

The AB 700 personal-account-search-duty theme surfaced by two panelists is undermined by their own inconsistent section citations (§ 7922.545 vs. § 6252.5, different chapter numbers), so I decline to lean on it as a doctrinal anchor without independent verification of the correct codification.

rounds.biggest_uncertainty: Whether a reviewing court treats the objective falsity of the "no responsive records" answer (given the existing 40 screenshots) as sufficient by itself to find a CPRA violation, or instead requires a separate finding that the clerk's search methodology was unreasonable at the time — and relatedly, whether any public-records holding would extend to the auto-deleted bulk of messages or be confined to the surviving screenshots, leaving the deleted portion to a retention/spoliation analysis outside the core CPRA violation finding.
rounds.response_to_panel: Two themes genuinely moved me upward from 76 to 79: (1) Theme D's near-unanimous framing (6/7) of the surviving screenshots as making the response factually false independent of search-methodology debates — this is a more robust violation theory than the one I emphasized in round 1, since it isn't vulnerable to the 'clerk lacked actual knowledge' defense I had flagged as my chief source of doubt; and (2) Theme H's point about majority-bloc (3-of-5) participation, which I had not weighted — it strengthens the public-business characterization beyond San Jose's own facts. I did not defer to the panel median mechanically; I declined to move higher despite the median/mean sitting at 78/76.9 because Theme I (scope-limiting risk, raised only by the two highest-probability panelists) persuades me there's a genuine residual chance a court partitions the holding between surviving and deleted messages, which cuts against treating the full proposition as clean. I also declined to adopt AB 700 as a supporting authority given the panel's own internal citation conflict (Disagreement point 5), treating that theme as too unverified to lean on.

rounds.position_summary: A reviewing court more likely than not holds both halves of the proposition: the Signal content is a "public record" under San Jose's content-based test because it substantively concerns pending public business (franchise terms, diversion targets, "what council will accept," RFP timing), and the clerk's "no responsive records" response — reached by searching only city systems despite a request expressly covering "any platform or account" and despite ~40 screenshots known to survive — falls short of a reasonably-calculated search and constitutes a CPRA violation. The weakest link remains prong two, since San Jose left search-methodology standards for case-by-case development, and two additional wrinkles (the third-party/ephemeral-platform character of the channel, and the possibility a court confines any "public records" holding to the surviving screenshots rather than the full deleted corpus) add genuine, if secondary, uncertainty to prong one that I had previously treated as close to settled.
rationales: San Jose's content-controls-not-location holding is essentially uncontested across the panel (7/7), and the described subject matter (franchise terms, diversion targets, 'what council will accept,' RFP timing) is core conduct-of-public-business content, not personal or incidental chatter, so prong one rests on a well-established doctrinal foundation.

Three of five councilmembers — a voting majority — participated in substantive deliberation, which several panelists (and I) treat as reinforcing both the content nexus and the 'prepared, owned, used, or retained' element under San Jose's agency-law reasoning; this is a genuine strength of the classification argument, not merely cumulative detail.

The ~40 surviving screenshots make 'no responsive records' arguably factually false at the moment it was given, independent of the auto-deletion issue — a point 6 of 7 panelists converged on independently, which I treat as a strong, largely uncontested basis for finding a violation on prong two regardless of how the search-methodology dispute is resolved.

Countervailing and largest source of uncertainty: San Jose expressly left search-methodology doctrine for case-by-case development, and the panel split on whether the request's 'any platform or account' language alone triggers a duty to inquire of each custodian or whether the clerk's lack of actual/constructive knowledge of the Signal group at the time of response supports a good-faith reasonable-search defense; I cannot resolve this doctrinal fork with confidence, and it is the crux on which prong two turns.

Two panel-raised wrinkles I had underweighted in round 1 modestly increase uncertainty on prong one: (a) the consultant-created, third-party-hosted, disappearing-message character of the channel — distinguishable from San Jose's own facts involving officials' own texts/emails — creates a genuinely open question about whether councilmembers 'used or retained' the writings in the relevant sense; and (b) a court could confine any affirmative 'public records' holding to the ~40 surviving screenshots and treat the bulk of deleted messages as a retention/spoliation question rather than resolving their public-record status outright, which would only partially validate the proposition as framed.
rounds.biggest_uncertainty: Whether the CPRA's search-adequacy standard is judged against the clerk's actual/constructive knowledge at the time of response (permitting a good-faith-search defense given no known awareness of the Signal group) or against an objective 'any platform or account' request-scope standard that itself imposes a duty to pre-emptively canvass every named custodian — San Jose left search methodology for case-specific development, and the panel's own citations to post-remand authority (e.g., the 2018 Court of Appeal decision) are disputed and unverified, so this doctrinal fork remains genuinely unresolved.

What did move me — modestly, down to 76 — was crediting two wrinkles I had previously treated as settled or minor: the third-party/ephemeral-platform distinction raised by the lowest-probability panelist (Theme L), and the possibility that a court confines its 'public records' holding to only the surviving screenshots rather than the full deleted corpus (Theme I, raised by the two highest-probability panelists but flagged by me as an underappreciated scope-limiting risk to the proposition as literally worded). Conversely, Theme H (majority-bloc participation) and the screenshot-falsity point (Theme D) reinforced rather than changed my existing reasoning, so they didn't move the number further. I did not adopt the disputed 2018 Court of Appeal citation (Disagreement point 2) in either direction since I cannot verify its actual holding and the panel itself split on what it stands for; I've flagged it for verification rather than let it push my estimate either way.

rounds.position_summary: Unchanged from round 1: more likely than not, and fairly strongly so, that a court holds both halves of the conjunctive proposition true — the Signal messages were "public records" under San Jose's content-based test, and the clerk's city-systems-only search (never contacting the three councilmembers despite a request explicitly reaching "any platform or account") violated the CPRA's reasonable-search obligation, independently confirmed by the known-but-undisclosed 40 surviving screenshots.
rationales: The panel's near-unanimous convergence (7/7) on the content-controls-not-location threshold and (6/7) on the screenshots-as-proof-of-falsity theory tracks my own reasoning exactly and reinforces rather than revises it — this is confirmatory, not new information, so it doesn't independently justify moving the number.

The 'clerk's lack of actual/constructive knowledge' defense (Theme G, 3/7) is a genuine countervailing consideration I take seriously, but I judge it doctrinally weaker than it first appears: the entire post-San Jose/AB 700 inquiry duty exists precisely because agencies often lack advance knowledge of personal-account communications — the trigger is the scope of the request ('any platform or account'), not the clerk's prior awareness of a specific Signal group. I don't think this defense, standing alone, should pull the probability down materially.

The doctrinal-state disagreement (point 2: what the 2018 Court of Appeal remand decision, 24 Cal.App.5th 1013, actually holds about search methodology) is a real source of added uncertainty I did not weigh explicitly in round 1 — if that decision does mandate custodian-level searches, it strengthens the second conjunct; if search methodology remains genuinely unresolved, the fact-bound framing (which I already flagged as my top uncertainty) gets more weight. I treat this as roughly a wash against the confirmatory themes above.

The third-party/consultant-hosted, ephemeral-by-design platform issue (Theme L, the panel's lowest scorer) remains a legitimate open question with no on-point published CA authority, but I still think it's a weaker distinction than San Jose's own facts suggest — the 'prepared, owned, used, or retained' element focuses on the official's relationship to the writing, not who built the app, so I don't discount for this beyond what I already built into my confidence level.

The deleted-messages-scope point (Theme I, the two highest scorers) doesn't move me either direction: even if a court confines its itemized public-record findings to the surviving screenshots, the proposition as worded is a categorical statement about 'the councilmembers' Signal messages concerning the franchise' that a court could satisfy through a general legal holding about the class of communications, without needing message-by-message proof for the deleted portion.

Code § 6252(e)/§ 7920.530, and discussion of franchise terms, diversion targets, and RFP timing among a voting majority of council maps closely onto San Jose's own personal-device fact pattern; I estimate this prong alone at roughly 88-90% likely to be found true.

The ~40 surviving screenshots supply a violation basis for the 'no responsive records' answer that is largely insulated from the harder doctrinal question of when a duty to inquire is triggered: regardless of whether the clerk had actual/constructive notice of the Signal group, records that indisputably existed in a councilmember's possession at the time of response were never located or produced — a comparatively clean, fact-bound basis for a violation finding that six of seven panelists independently converged on.

The compound structure of the proposition (both public-record status AND a violation finding must hold) is real and was the panel's most consistently flagged risk (5/7 panelists), but multiplying my independent estimates for each prong (~89% and ~85%, with positive correlation since the same aggressive-compliance reasoning tends to drive both findings together) lands close to 78-80%, confirming rather than undercutting my round-1 estimate.

The panel's disagreement over the doctrinal trigger for the duty to inquire (request language alone vs. actual/constructive knowledge of the platform) and over whether post-San Jose search-methodology law is settled (Disagreement points 1-2) restates, rather than adds to, the uncertainty I already identified as my round-1 'biggest uncertainty' — so it confirms my calibration without providing new information that should move me further.

A CPRA public-record determination attaches at creation based on content and the 'prepared, owned, used, or retained' elements, not on continued existence; declaratory relief does not require that responsive records currently exist (Theme J), which weakens any argument that auto-deletion of most messages should defeat the public-records characterization of the whole category rather than merely create a separate retention/spoliation problem for remedy purposes.

summaries:
# Delphi Panel — Round 1 Feedback Summary

## 1. Statistics

Panel of 7. Probability that the proposition is correct: median 78.0, mean 76.9, std dev 3.2, range 70.0–80.0, middle 50% of panel between 76.0 and 80.0.

## 2. Rationale Themes

**Theme A — Content-controls-not-location (San Jose prong one).** *7 of 7 panelists.* Every panelist grounded the "public record" determination in *City of San Jose*'s content-based test, and every panelist characterized the subject matter (franchise terms, diversion targets, "what council will accept," RFP timing) as core conduct of the public's business.

**Theme B — Duty to inquire of individual custodians / reasonable-search standard.** *7 of 7 panelists.* All invoked some version of a reasonably-calculated or reasonable-diligence search standard and noted the clerk's failure to ask any of the three councilmembers about personal-account communications. Panelists differed on the doctrinal source (San Jose remand instructions, § 6253.1/§ 7922.600 duty-to-assist, AB 700, case law on search adequacy) — see Theme E and Disagreement point 2.

**Theme C — "Any platform or account" request language as notice.** *5 of 7 panelists* (probabilities 70, 76, 78, 78, 80). These panelists argued the request's express wording put the clerk on notice that a city-systems-only search was insufficient, with some describing the resulting search as "facially inadequate" or a candidate for a per-se violation.

**Theme D — The ~40 surviving screenshots.** *7 of 7 panelists, but used in two distinct ways:*
- *6 panelists* (76 through 80) treated the screenshots as making "no responsive records" factually inaccurate at the moment it was given — an independent basis for a violation regardless of the deleted messages.
- *1 panelist* (70) used the screenshots differently: as evidentiary proof of content and participation that allows a court to reach a definitive holding on the public-record question, rather than as proof of response falsity.

**Theme E — AB 700 statutory codification of personal-account search duties.** *2 of 7 panelists* (76, 78) cited AB 700 as codifying an affirmative duty to route requests to individual officials — though with different section numbers and chapter citations (see Disagreement point 5).

**Theme F — Conjunctive fragility of the proposition.** *5 of 7 panelists* (70, 76, 78, 78, 80) expressly noted that the proposition requires both prongs to hold, making it more fragile than either prong alone; the second (search-adequacy) prong was uniformly identified as the weaker link among these panelists.

**Theme G — Clerk's lack of actual/constructive knowledge as a countervailing defense.** *3 of 7 panelists* (76, 76, 78) — a mid-range cluster — flagged that a court could excuse the initial search as reasonable if the clerk had no actual or constructive notice of the Signal group at the time of response.

**Theme H — Majority-bloc participation strengthens the public-business nexus.** *3 of 7 panelists* (78, 80, 80) — skewing toward higher-probability panelists — emphasized that three of five councilmembers (a voting majority) participating in substantive deliberation reinforces both the content nexus and the "prepared, owned, used, or retained" element.

**Theme I — Deleted-messages scope problem.** *2 of 7 panelists* (both at 80 — the highest-probability panelists) raised the possibility that a court might limit its "public records" holding to the surviving screenshots and treat the bulk of auto-deleted messages as a retention/spoliation issue rather than a CPRA determination.

**Theme J — Availability of declaratory relief despite destruction.** *1 panelist* (76) noted that CPRA declaratory/injunctive relief does not require that responsive records presently exist.

**Theme K — Possible exemptions even if records qualify.** *1 panelist* (76) noted content could be found preliminary/deliberative and exempt from disclosure despite public-record status.

**Theme L — Third-party/consultant-hosted platform distinction.** *1 panelist* (70 — the lowest-probability panelist) flagged that San Jose involved the official's own texts/emails, whereas this involves a private bidder's consultant-run disappearing-message platform, with no published California decision on point.

## 3. Points of Genuine Disagreement

1. **What triggers the duty to inquire of individual officials.** Some panelists treat the request's "any platform or account" language as itself sufficient to trigger the duty (making the city-systems-only search per se or facially inadequate); others posit that absent actual or constructive knowledge of the Signal group, the search could be found reasonable "at the time." Several panelists hold both positions in tension within their own answers, but as between panelists the trigger assumption is incompatible: request-language-alone versus knowledge-required.

2. **The state of post-San Jose search-methodology doctrine.** The 70 panelist cites the 2018 Court of Appeal remand decision (*City of San Jose v. Superior Court* (2018) 24 Cal.App.5th 1013) as published authority contemplating that agencies must ask custodians to search personal accounts. Other panelists (78, 78, 80) describe search methodology as expressly left unresolved by San Jose, with no binding appellate authority fixing the duty's contours. These are incompatible readings of the same doctrinal landscape.

3. **Scope of any "public records" holding as to the deleted messages.** The two 80 panelists assume a court might confine its holding to the ~40 surviving screenshots and recharacterize the rest as a retention/spoliation matter; other panelists' analyses assume the full body of Signal messages is subject to the public-record determination.

4. **Whether the Signal platform's characteristics matter.** The 70 panelist treats the consultant-initiated, third-party-hosted, ephemeral nature of the channel as a genuine open question under the "prepared, owned, used, or retained" element; other panelists (78, 80) describe these facts as a *cleaner* fit for San Jose than San Jose's own facts, treating the platform's characteristics as immaterial.

5. **What Gov. Code § 7922.545 is.** One panelist (76) cites § 7922.545 as the AB 700 personal-account search provision (Stats. 2021, ch. 132); another panelist (76) cites § 7922.545 as the recodified preliminary-drafts exemption (former § 6254(a)); a third panelist (78) cites the AB 700 provision as § 6252.5 (Stats. 2021, ch. 456). These citations are mutually inconsistent as to section number and chapter, and multiple panelists flagged their own citations for verification.

6. **Per se violation versus fact-bound adequacy question.** Panelists split on whether the "no responsive records" response would be treated as a clean statutory violation or as a fact-intensive search-adequacy question on which the city could partially prevail through a good-faith showing.

# Persistent Disagreement Report — Final Round

**Numeric context first:** the panel converged tightly (76–79, σ = 1.0). The persistent disagreement is therefore not about the bottom-line probability but about *which reasoning pathway carries the estimate* and *where residual risk lives*. Four substantive fault lines and one epistemic one survived mutual feedback.

---

## Fault Line 1: What triggers the duty to inquire of individual custodians

**(a) Cruxes.**
- One camp rests on the position that the request's express "any platform or account" language *itself* obligated the clerk to route inquiries to the named councilmembers — the trigger is request scope, not agency awareness. Several panelists explicitly re-derived their prong-two estimates on this basis.
- The other camp rests on the possibility that a court requires the clerk's *actual or constructive knowledge* of the Signal group before deeming a city-systems-only search unreasonable, preserving a good-faith-search defense.

**(b) Source of split.** A genuinely unsettled legal question. Multiple panelists independently stated that *San Jose* left search methodology for case-by-case development and that no binding published appellate authority fixes the trigger. This is the panel's most cleanly identified doctrinal fork; it was flagged as the "biggest uncertainty" by a majority of panelists even after convergence.

**(c) What would resolve it.** A published California appellate decision (or verified reading of existing post-remand authority) squarely holding either that broad request language alone compels custodian-level inquiry, or that some threshold of agency awareness is required. Alternatively, a definitive construction of the AB 700-derived personal-account provision (see Fault Line 5).

---

## Fault Line 2: Does the screenshot-falsity theory stand alone, or collapse into search adequacy?

**(a) Cruxes.**
- Most panelists (and the largest round-over-round movers) rest prong two on the claim that "no responsive records" was *objectively false when given* — ~40 responsive screenshots existed on a councilmember's device — and that this factual falsity establishes a violation *independent of* the clerk's diligence or knowledge.
- A minority position holds that a court might not treat objective falsity as a freestanding violation, instead folding everything into a fact-bound search-adequacy inquiry in which the clerk's good faith and lack of knowledge would be credited.

**(b) Source of split.** Primarily an unsettled legal question (is a factually inaccurate response a violation per se, or only if the search producing it was unreasonable?), with a secondary factual assumption embedded: that the screenshots still existed on the personal device at the time of the response. Panelists uniformly treated that assumption as near-certain but acknowledged it is inferred, not stipulated.

**(c) What would resolve it.** Judicially: a holding on whether CPRA violation findings attach to response accuracy or only to search reasonableness. Factually: confirmation of when the screenshots existed relative to the response date.

---

## Fault Line 3: Scope of any "public records" holding — the class vs. the surviving screenshots

**(a) Cruxes.**
- Some panelists (including those who discounted for this) rest on the risk that a court confines an affirmative public-records holding to the ~40 surviving screenshots and routes the deleted bulk into a retention/spoliation lane — making the proposition, as literally worded ("the councilmembers' Signal messages... were public records"), only *partially* validated.
- Others rest on the reading that the proposition poses a *characterization* question about the class of communications, which a court would answer categorically (as *San Jose* announced a governing principle), with any screenshot-confinement going to remedy, not characterization. One panelist added that public-record status attaches at creation and declaratory relief does not require records to presently exist.

**(b) Source of split.** A mix of (i) an interpretive disagreement about the proposition's wording (characterization vs. remedy framing) and (ii) an unsettled legal question about how courts treat destroyed records within a CPRA determination. Notably, panelists disagreed about whether this risk should *lower* the estimate or is orthogonal to it — some explicitly declined to discount for it while others explicitly did.

**(c) What would resolve it.** A court order in the hypothetical case showing whether the public-records finding is framed categorically or item-by-item; or, upstream, clarification of how the proposition itself should be scored if a court splits the holding.

---

## Fault Line 4: The consultant-created, third-party-hosted, auto-deleting platform

**(a) Cruxes.**
- The minority position rests on the absence of on-point published authority for whether officials "used or retained" writings in a channel they neither created nor hosted, with messages ephemeral by design — a fact pattern distinguishable from *San Jose*'s own facts (officials' own texts/emails).
- The majority position rests on *San Jose*'s test being content-based and platform-agnostic: the "prepared, owned, used, or retained" element concerns the official's relationship to the writing, not who built or configured the channel.

**(b) Source of split.** An unsettled legal question of narrow scope. Even panelists who dismissed it acknowledged no published California authority addresses it directly; the disagreement is over how much weight a court would give the distinction, which shades into differing risk tolerance about novel-fact-pattern litigation.

**(c) What would resolve it.** Authority (or a ruling in this case) addressing whether participation in a third-party-controlled ephemeral channel satisfies "used or retained."

---

## Fault Line 5 (epistemic): Contested and unverified authorities

Two citation disputes persisted unresolved and were handled divergently:

- **The 2018 post-remand Court of Appeal decision (cited as 24 Cal.App.5th 1013):** panelists split on whether it fixes a custodian-level search rule or leaves methodology open. Several explicitly declined to lean on it in either direction pending verification; none claimed to have verified it.
- **The AB 700 personal-account-inquiry provision:** panelists cited inconsistent section numbers (§ 7922.545 vs. § 6252.5) and chapter numbers, and disagreed about whether its trigger is request scope or agency awareness. At least two panelists expressly declined to use it as a doctrinal anchor because of the internal citation conflict.

**(b) Source of split.** Not doctrine or risk tolerance — a *research-verification gap*. The panel could not confirm what these authorities actually say.

**(c) What would resolve it.** Straightforward legal research: pulling the actual 2018 opinion and the enacted AB 700 text/codification. This is the only fault line resolvable without a new judicial decision or new facts.

---

## Summary

| Fault line | Nature of split | Resolvable by |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Duty-to-inquire trigger | Unsettled law | New appellate holding or verified statute |
| 2. Falsity as standalone violation | Unsettled law + one factual assumption | Holding on violation theory; screenshot-timing facts |
| 3. Class vs. screenshots scope | Proposition interpretation + unsettled law | How the court frames its holding; scoring clarification |
| 4. Third-party ephemeral platform | Unsettled law / risk tolerance | On-point authority on "used or retained" |
| 5. Contested citations | Verification gap | Ordinary legal research |

The panel's residual 3-point spread maps almost entirely onto Fault Lines 1–3: panelists at the low end of the range weight the knowledge-based search defense and the scope-narrowing risk more heavily; panelists at the high end rest on the falsity theory's independence from the unresolved trigger question. No panelist changed camps on any fault line in the final round; movement occurred only in weighting.
